<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Internet of Things (IoT). List any FOUR characteristics of IoT.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** IoT is a network of uniquely identifiable physical objects embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity that enables these objects to **collect, exchange, and act upon** data over the Internet without requiring human intervention.
>
> **Four Characteristics (0.375 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Connectivity** — Devices connect via Wi-Fi, 5G, BLE, LoRa, etc.
> 2. **Intelligence & Identity** — Each device has a unique IP and embedded intelligence.
> 3. **Scalability** — Can scale from a few devices to billions.
> 4. **Dynamic & Self-Adapting** — Adapts to changing context and environment.
> 5. **Heterogeneity** — Different hardware/network platforms coexist.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between IoT and M2M (Machine-to-Machine) communication.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Parameter | M2M | IoT |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Point-to-point, localized | Global, Internet-scale |
| **Protocol** | Proprietary, vendor-specific | IP-based, open standards |
| **Intelligence** | Pre-programmed rules | AI/ML-driven analytics |
| **Data Destination** | Local SCADA/server | Cloud / edge hybrid |
| **Human Role** | Minimal (machine-only) | Human-in-the-loop via apps |

*(Any 4 valid distinctions carry 3 marks.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

> **KTU Module Choice Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question (either A or B). Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`
**(a)** Explain the **5-layer architecture of IoT** with a neat diagram. Describe the function of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **any three communication protocols** used in IoT and compare them based on range, power consumption, and data rate. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Perception Layer (1 Mark):** The physical layer where sensors detect real-world phenomena like temperature, pressure, motion, and convert them into electrical/digital signals. Examples: DHT22 temperature sensor, MPU6050 accelerometer, RFID tags.
2. **Network Layer (1.5 Marks):** Handles data routing and transmission. Uses protocols like Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRaWAN, ZigBee. Responsible for addressing, packetization, and forwarding.
3. **Middleware Layer (1.5 Marks):** Performs data aggregation, filtering, storage, and analytics. Includes edge gateways, MQTT brokers, and databases (e.g., InfluxDB, MongoDB).
4. **Application Layer (1.5 Marks):** Delivers user-facing services like smart home apps, industrial dashboards, and alerting systems. Translates processed data into actionable insights.
5. **Business Layer (1.5 Marks):** Manages the overall system — ROI, strategy, lifecycle, and decision support. Aligns IoT operations with business objectives using analytics tools like Power BI, Grafana.

*(Include the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 for the remaining 0.5 mark.)*

---

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Protocol 1 — Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):**
- Range: ~100 m
- Power: High
- Data Rate: up to 600 Mbps (Wi-Fi 6)
- Use: Indoor smart home, video streaming cameras.

**Protocol 2 — Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE):**
- Range: ~10 m
- Power: Ultra-low (~0.01–0.5 W)
- Data Rate: 2 Mbps
- Use: Wearables, beacons, health monitors.

**Protocol 3 — LoRaWAN:**
- Range: ~15 km
- Power: Very low (~0.001 W)
- Data Rate: 0.3–50 kbps
- Use: Smart agriculture, smart metering.

*(2.3 Marks per protocol + 0.1 for comparative summary table = 7 Marks.)*

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** What are the **four pillars of IoT**? Explain each with industrial examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete data flow in an IoT system** from sensor to actuator. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Identification (1.75 Marks):** Each "thing" needs a unique ID. Example: A smart city's water pipeline gets an **RFID tag + IPv6 address** so maintenance crews can pinpoint leaks among **10,000+ nodes** within seconds.
2. **Sensing (1.75 Marks):** Sensors collect real-time data. Example: **Bosch BMP280** pressure sensors deployed in aircraft cabin to monitor altitude pressure differential.
3. **Communication (1.75 Marks):** Data is transmitted via protocols. Example: **NB-IoT** used by electricity boards for smart meters that send hourly readings from remote villages.
4. **Computation (1.75 Marks):** Cloud + AI processes the data. Example: **Google Cloud IoT** analyzing **petabytes of factory data** to predict equipment failures using LSTM neural networks.

---

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

The data flow follows these **six stages** (1 Mark each + 1 Mark for diagram):

1. **Sensing:** Sensor captures physical signal (e.g., thermistor reads temperature).
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifier + ADC convert analog to digital.
3. **Local Processing:** MCU computes statistics and detects anomalies.
4. **Transmission:** Data packet sent via Wi-Fi/5G to gateway.
5. **Cloud Processing:** AI/ML inference decides if action is needed.
6. **Actuation:** Command sent back to actuator (motor/valve) to respond.

*(Include the Mermaid flow from Section 4.2 for the diagram mark.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IoT Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing M2M with IoT** — Many students write both as the same. M2M is a *subset* of IoT focused on direct device-to-device comms; IoT is the broader Internet-scaled ecosystem.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark answers** — KTU mandates a *"neat diagram"* for full marks. Even a simple block diagram earns **at least 1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Memorizing layers without explaining functions** — Just listing the 5 layers gets only 2–3 marks. You **must** describe *what each layer does* with examples.
> 4. **Forgetting the Business Layer** — Many students stop at Application Layer. The **Business Layer is mandatory** in the 5-layer model.
> 5. **Not citing real-world protocols** — Avoid generic answers like *"it uses wireless"*; always cite **specific protocols (MQTT, CoAP, LoRaWAN, BLE)**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - ✅ **IoT Definition:** Network of uniquely identifiable things with sensors, software, and connectivity that exchange data over the Internet.
> - ✅ **Origin:** Coined by **Kevin Ashton in 1999** at **Procter & Gamble**.
> - ✅ **5-Layer Model (ITU-T Y.2060):** Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business.
> - ✅ **Pillars of IoT:** Identification, Sensing, Communication, Computation.
> - ✅ **M2M ≠ IoT** — M2M is the foundation; IoT adds IP-connectivity, cloud intelligence, and scalability.
> - ✅ **IoT vs CPS:** IoT focuses on **connectivity and data exchange**; CPS focuses on **computation, control, and physical-world feedback loops**.
> - ✅ **Key Protocols:** MQTT (lightweight pub/sub), CoAP (constrained REST), HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP, DDS.
> - ✅ **Standard Identifier:** **IPv6** (128-bit) is preferred over IPv4 due to massive address space.
> - ✅ **Industry 4.0 Linkage:** IoT is one of the **four pillars of Industry 4.0** (IoT, CPS, AI, Cloud).
> - ✅ **High-Yield Buzzwords:** Ambient Intelligence, Ubiquitous Computing, Edge Computing, Digital Twin, M2M, OTA.
> - ✅ **Real-World Examples:** Smart homes (Alexa), smart agriculture (John Deere), smart healthcare (remote patient monitoring), smart manufacturing (Siemens MindSphere).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors, Actuators, and IoT Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT754 - INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors, Actuators, and IoT Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Sensor?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT754, Module 2):**
> A **sensor** is a physical device that detects, measures, and responds to some type of input from the physical environment. The specific input could be **light, heat, motion, moisture, pressure, vibration, or any one of a great number of other environmental phenomena**. The output is generally a **signal that is converted to a human-readable display** at the sensor location or transmitted electronically over a network for reading or further processing.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Analogy:**
Think of your own body. Your skin feels heat, your eyes see light, your ears hear sound, your tongue tastes, and your nose smells. All these **five senses** are your biological *sensors*. They convert real-world physical stimuli (heat, light, sound, chemicals) into **electrical nerve impulses** that your brain interprets. An industrial sensor does the exact same job — it converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, force) into a **standardized electrical signal** (voltage, current, resistance, frequency, or digital code) that a controller can read.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors vs. Transducers:**
> In strict KTU terminology, a **transducer** is a broader term — *any device that converts one form of energy into another*. A **sensor** is a *specialized transducer whose primary function is to detect/measure a physical quantity*. Thus, *all sensors are transducers, but not all transducers are sensors* (an actuator is also a transducer, but in the reverse direction).

---

### 1.2 What is an Actuator?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An **actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that **converts electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy into motion or force**. It is the component responsible for **executing** a command — physically moving, controlling, or manipulating a mechanism or system in response to a control signal received from a controller (like a PLC or microcontroller).

**Conceptual Analogy — Muscles of the System:**
If sensors are the *senses*, then actuators are the **muscles**. When your brain decides to pick up a glass, your muscles (actuators) contract and move your arm to execute the action. In Industry 4.0, when a controller decides that a valve must open, an electric motor (actuator) physically turns the valve. The actuator takes an **electrical signal** and produces **physical motion** — torque, linear displacement, angular rotation, or force.

---

### 1.3 What is an IoT Device?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An **Internet of Things (IoT) device** is a piece of hardware — sensors, actuators, software, and other technologies — that **connects to the internet or other devices and exchanges data over a network**. In the industrial context (called the **Industrial Internet of Things, IIoT**), these devices form a connected, intelligent network enabling *real-time monitoring, automation, predictive maintenance, and data-driven decision-making*.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Nervous System:**
Imagine your body's nervous system. Your senses (sensors) detect, your spinal cord and brain (gateway + cloud) process, and your muscles (actuators) act. The *communication lines* (nerves) connect everything. An IoT device is the **modern technological equivalent of a nerve cell + sense organ + muscle + communication radio**, all packaged in a single chip. For example, an **ESP32** with a DHT22 sensor and a relay is an IoT device that senses humidity, transmits it to the cloud, and switches an exhaust fan (actuator) on its own.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Functional Metrics (for Sensors):**
> - **Accuracy** = closeness of measurement to the *true value* (expressed in **% of Full Scale (%FS)**).
> - **Precision** = closeness of *repeated measurements* to each other (reproducibility).
> - **Sensitivity** = the *smallest input change* that produces a detectable output change.
> - **Resolution** = the *smallest increment* the sensor can discriminate (e.g., 0.1 °C).
> - **Range** = the *minimum to maximum* input the sensor can measure (Span = Max − Min).

**Role in Industry 4.0:** Sensors, Actuators, and IoT devices form the **Physical Layer (Perception Layer)** of the Industry 4.0 reference architecture. Without them, there is no real-time data, no closed-loop control, no digital twin, and no cyber-physical production system (CPPS).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Sensors

Sensors can be classified along multiple axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus demands knowledge of all the following taxonomies:

**(a) By Output Signal Type:**
| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Analog Sensor** | Output is a continuous signal proportional to the input | Thermocouple, RTD, LVDT |
| **Digital Sensor** | Output is a discrete digital word | DS18B20 temperature sensor, encoders |
| **Binary Sensor** | Output is ON/OFF only | Limit switch, proximity switch, reed switch |

**(b) By Power Requirement:**
| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Active Sensor** | Self-generates electrical signal; no external power needed | Thermocouple (Seebeck effect), Piezoelectric, Photovoltaic cell |
| **Passive Sensor** | Requires external excitation (voltage/current) | RTD, Strain gauge, Thermistor, LDR |

**(c) By Detection Phenomenon:**
- **Physical Sensors:** Temperature, Pressure, Force, Flow, Level, Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Humidity, Light, Proximity.
- **Chemical Sensors:** pH, Gas (CO₂, CH₄, O₂), Humidity, Moisture.
- **Biological Sensors:** Glucose, DNA, Enzyme-based biosensors.

**(d) By Application Domain (Industry 4.0 Specific):**
- **Process Sensors** (used in process industries: oil & gas, chemical) — pressure transmitters, flow meters, level sensors.
- **Motion / Position Sensors** (used in robotics & CNC) — encoders, resolvers, accelerometers, gyroscopes, LiDAR.
- **Vision Sensors** (Industry 4.0 machine vision) — CCD/CMOS cameras, 3-D scanners.
- **Wireless / Smart Sensors** — sensors with onboard processing + wireless communication (IEEE 1451 standard).

---

### 2.2 Classification of Actuators

**(a) By Energy Domain:**
| Type | Working Principle | Typical Application |
|------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Hydraulic Actuator** | Uses pressurized oil (typically up to **35 MPa**) | Heavy machinery, presses, injection moulding |
| **Pneumatic Actuator** | Uses compressed air (typically **0.4–0.8 MPa**) | Pick-and-place, valves, food industry |
| **Electric Actuator** | Uses electromagnetic energy | Robotics, CNC, conveyor belts, valves |
| **Thermal Actuator** | Uses thermal expansion | Micro-actuators (MEMS), shape-memory alloys |
| **Piezoelectric Actuator** | Uses reverse piezoelectric effect | Nanopositioning, inkjet print heads |
| **Mechanical Actuator** | Uses gears, cams, levers | Clutches, brakes, screw drives |
| **Soft / Smart Actuator** | Uses EAP (electro-active polymers) or SMA | Soft robotics, biomedical |

**(b) Electric Actuator Sub-Types:**
- **DC Motor:** Continuous rotation; speed proportional to voltage.
- **Stepper Motor:** Discrete angular steps; open-loop position control.
- **Servo Motor:** Closed-loop position control using encoder feedback.
- **Solenoid:** Linear push-pull motion from electromagnetic coil.

---

### 2.3 Architecture of an IoT Device (3-Layer / 5-Layer Model)

A typical IoT device has the following building blocks:
1. **Sensing / Actuation Subsystem** — physical interface to the world.
2. **Processing Subsystem** — microcontroller (MCU) / microprocessor (e.g., Arduino UNO — ATmega328P @ 16 MHz; ESP32 — Xtensa LX6 dual-core @ 240 MHz; Raspberry Pi — Broadcom BCM2711 quad-core Cortex-A72 @ 1.5 GHz).
3. **Communication Subsystem** — radio module (Wi-Fi IEEE 802.11 b/g/n, Bluetooth Low Energy 5.0, ZigBee IEEE 802.15.4, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, MQTT over TCP/IP).
4. **Power Subsystem** — battery (Li-Po 3.7 V), energy harvesting, or mains adapter.
5. **Application / Cloud Interface** — firmware, RTOS (FreeRTOS, Contiki), cloud SDK (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every row is a potential Part-A (3-mark) or Part-B (14-mark) question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y_{out}}{\Delta x_{in}}$ | V/Unit of input | Slope of the calibration curve |
| 2 | **Nonlinearity (%FS)** | $\%NL = \dfrac{\Delta L_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Max deviation from best-fit straight line, normalized by Full Scale |
| 3 | **Hysteresis (%FS)** | $\%H = \dfrac{\Delta H_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Max difference between up-scale and down-scale readings at the same input |
| 4 | **Repeatability (%FS)** | $\%R = \dfrac{\Delta R_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Closeness of successive measurements under identical conditions |
| 5 | **Accuracy** | $\%A = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert e_{max} \vert}{Y_{true}}\right) \times 100$ | dimensionless | $e_{max}$ = maximum error |
| 6 | **Dead Band** | $DB = x_{out} - x_{in,start}$ | input units | Smallest input change producing no output change |
| 7 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | Higher = cleaner signal |
| 8 | **Transfer Function (linear)** | $y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$ | depends on $K$ | $K$ = static sensitivity |
| 9 | **First-Order Step Response** | $y(t) = K A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | depends on $K,A$ | $\tau$ = time constant (63.2% of final value) |
| 10 | **Thermocouple (Seebeck)** | $V_{out} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | V (or $\mu$V) | $\alpha$ = Seebeck coefficient ($\mu$V/°C) |
| 11 | **RTD (linear approx.)** | $R(T) = R_{0}\!\left[1 + \alpha\,(T - T_{0})\right]$ | $\Omega$ | $\alpha \approx 0.00385$ /°C for Platinum (Pt100) |
| 12 | **Thermistor (Steinhart-Hart)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = a + b \ln R + c (\ln R)^{3}$ | K | $T$ = absolute temperature |
| 13 | **Strain Gauge (Gauge Factor)** | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | $\varepsilon$ = strain = $\Delta L / L$ |
| 14 | **Piezoelectric Sensor** | $Q = d \cdot F$ | C (Coulomb) | $d$ = charge sensitivity (pC/N) |
| 15 | **LVDT Sensitivity** | $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | V/m or V/inch | $K$ = LVDT sensitivity constant |
| 16 | **Pressure (general)** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | 1 bar = $10^{5}$ Pa |
| 17 | **DC Motor Speed** | $\omega = \dfrac{V_{a} - I_{a} R_{a}}{K_{e}}$ | rad/s | $K_{e}$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) |
| 18 | **DC Motor Torque** | $\tau = K_{t} \cdot I_{a}$ | N·m | $K_{t}$ = torque constant (N·m/A); in SI, $K_{t} = K_{e}$ |
| 19 | **Stepper Step Angle** | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}}{N}$ | degrees | $N$ = total number of steps per revolution |
| 20 | **Force from Acceleration** | $F = m \cdot a$ | N | Newton's 2nd law |
| 21 | **Heat Transfer (Conduction)** | $Q = \dfrac{kA\,\Delta T}{L}$ | W | Fourier's law, $k$ = thermal conductivity |
| 22 | **Radiative Heat Transfer** | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_{1}^{4} - T_{2}^{4})$ | W | Stefan-Boltzmann, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) |
| 23 | **Optical Encoder Resolution** | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}}{N_{pulses}}$ | degrees/pulse | $N_{pulses}$ = pulses per revolution (PPR) |
| 24 | **Capacitive Sensor** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A}{d}$ | F | Change in $A$, $d$, or $\varepsilon_{r}$ gives sensing |
| 25 | **Inductive Sensor (LVDT)** | $V_{out} \propto \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ | V | Only detects *dynamic* displacement unless rectified |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL MARK-LOSS WARNING:** In KTU valuation, students often forget to **express Hysteresis, Nonlinearity, and Repeatability as %FS** (normalized by Full Scale output), not in absolute output units. Always normalize!

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Sensors Used | Actuator Used |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Automotive Engine Control (ECU)** | MAP sensor, O₂ sensor, MAF, crankshaft position, knock | Fuel injector (solenoid), throttle body (DC servo), EGR valve |
| **Smart Factory CNC** | Linear encoder, force/torque sensor, vibration sensor | Stepper / Servo motor, hydraulic ram |
| **Predictive Maintenance (IIoT)** | Vibration (accelerometer), acoustic emission, temperature | Maintenance crew alerted via cloud dashboard (no actuator, but trigger event) |
| **Pharma Cold Chain** | RTD (Pt100), humidity sensor | Peltier cooler (TEC) for active temperature control |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Soil moisture, NPK, pH, DHT22 (T & RH) | Solenoid valve for irrigation, DC pump |

---

### 2.6 The Smart Sensor Concept (Industry 4.0 Specific)

A **smart sensor** (per IEEE 1451 standard) integrates four functions:
1. **Signal conditioning** (amplification, filtering, linearization).
2. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)**.
3. **Local intelligence** (microprocessor for calibration, compensation, self-test).
4. **Digital communication interface** (I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, Modbus, MQTT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Smart"?** Because unlike a passive transducer, a smart sensor can:
> - Auto-calibrate against a reference.
> - Compensate for temperature drift.
> - Run self-diagnostics (BITE — Built-In Test Equipment).
> - Transmit digitally via industrial protocols.
>
> Example: **BME680** (Bosch) — measures T, P, RH, and VOC gas in one chip with I²C/SPI interface.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sensor Transfer Function and Linearity Error

Consider a temperature sensor with a non-linear calibration curve. Let the measured input-output data be:
- $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)$ where $x_i$ is temperature in °C and $y_i$ is voltage in V.

We fit a **best-fit straight line** (least-squares regression) of the form:

$$
y = a + b\,x
$$

where $b$ = slope = static sensitivity $K$, and $a$ = offset (intercept).

**Least-squares estimates of $a$ and $b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \dfrac{n \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i y_i) - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i\right)}{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{2} - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^{2}} \\[8pt]
a &= \dfrac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Linearity (Nonlinearity) Error at any point $x_i$:**

$$
\Delta L_i = y_{i,actual} - y_{i,line}
$$

where $y_{i,line} = a + b\,x_i$.

**Maximum Nonlinearity:**

$$
\Delta L_{max} = \max_i \vert \Delta L_i \vert
$$

**% Nonlinearity (%NL):**

$$
\%NL = \dfrac{\Delta L_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100
$$

where $Y_{FS} = y_{max} - y_{min}$ is the full-scale output.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Hysteresis Error Calculation

> **Problem:** A pressure transducer is calibrated on both increasing and decreasing pressure cycles. The data is tabulated below. Compute the **Hysteresis in %FS**.
>
> | Pressure (bar) | Output Up-scale (V) | Output Down-scale (V) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
> | 2 | 1.02 | 0.98 |
> | 4 | 2.00 | 1.95 |
> | 6 | 3.05 | 3.00 |
> | 8 | 4.00 | 3.96 |
> | 10 | 5.00 | 5.00 |

**Step 1 — Compute Full-Scale Output ($Y_{FS}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{FS} &= y_{max} - y_{min} \\
&= 5.00 - 0.00 = 5.00 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Hysteresis at Each Pressure Point:**

The hysteresis at each point is the difference between up-scale and down-scale readings:

$$
\Delta H_i = y_{i,up} - y_{i,down}
$$

| $P$ (bar) | Up (V) | Down (V) | $\Delta H_i$ (V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 1.02 | 0.98 | 0.04 |
| 4 | 2.00 | 1.95 | 0.05 |
| 6 | 3.05 | 3.00 | 0.05 |
| 8 | 4.00 | 3.96 | 0.04 |
| 10 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 0.00 |

**Step 3 — Identify Maximum Hysteresis:**

$$
\Delta H_{max} = \max_i \Delta H_i = 0.05 \text{ V} \quad \text{(at } P = 4 \text{ bar and } P = 6 \text{ bar)}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as %FS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%H &= \dfrac{\Delta H_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.05}{5.00} \times 100 = 1.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** Hysteresis = **1.0 %FS**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: LVDT Sensitivity and Output Voltage

An **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive sensor used to measure linear displacement.

**Construction:** A primary coil (P) is sandwiched between two identical secondary coils (S₁ and S₂), connected in series-opposition. A ferromagnetic core moves linearly inside the assembly.

**Operation:**
- When core is at **null (center)** position: $V_{S1} = V_{S2}$, so $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = 0$.
- When core moves toward S₁: $V_{S1} > V_{S2}$, so $V_{out} > 0$ (positive).
- When core moves toward S₂: $V_{S2} > V_{S1}$, so $V_{out} < 0$ (negative).

**Mutual Inductance Relation:**

$$
V_{out}(t) = -M_{1}\,\dfrac{di_{P}}{dt} + M_{2}\,\dfrac{di_{P}}{dt}
$$

For sinusoidal excitation $i_{P}(t) = I_{P}\sin(\omega t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{di_{P}}{dt} &= \omega I_{P}\cos(\omega t) \\
V_{out}(t) &= \omega I_{P}\cos(\omega t)\,(M_{2} - M_{1})
\end{aligned}
$$

The mutual inductances are linear functions of core position $x$:

$$
M_{1} = M_{0} - k\,x, \quad M_{2} = M_{0} + k\,x
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out}(t) &= \omega I_{P}\cos(\omega t)\,\big[(M_{0} + kx) - (M_{0} - kx)\big] \\
&= 2 k \omega I_{P}\, x \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **RMS output voltage** is therefore:

$$
V_{out,RMS} = K \cdot x, \quad \text{where } K = 2\sqrt{2}\,k\,\omega I_{P}
$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$
S_{LVDT} = \dfrac{V_{out,RMS}}{x} = K \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{V}}{\text{mm}}\right]
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Thermocouple Output Using Seebeck Effect

When two dissimilar metals (e.g., Chromel and Alumel — **Type K** thermocouple) are joined at a *hot junction* ($T_H$) and the other ends are held at a *cold junction* ($T_C$), an EMF is generated.

**Seebeck EMF:**

$$
V_{out} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \big[\alpha_{B}(T) - \alpha_{A}(T)\big]\,dT
$$

For Type K, over a wide temperature range, the Seebeck coefficient is approximately constant:
- $\alpha_{K} \approx 41 \text{ }\mu\text{V/°C}$ (in the 0–500 °C range).

Therefore:

$$
V_{out} \approx \alpha_{K} \cdot (T_H - T_C)
$$

**Worked Example:**
If hot junction is at $T_H = 250\,°C$ and cold junction compensation gives an effective $T_C = 25\,°C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= 41 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (250 - 25) \\
&= 41 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 225 \\
&= 9.225 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \\
&= 9.225 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC):** A thermocouple measures **differential temperature**, not absolute. Modern systems use a semiconductor IC (e.g., **MAX31855**) to measure the cold-junction temperature with an internal sensor and add the equivalent mV digitally.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Stepper Motor Step Angle and Holding Torque

A stepper motor's step angle is determined by the number of stator poles ($N_{p}$) and rotor teeth ($N_{r}$):

$$
\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}}{N_{p} \cdot N_{r}}
$$

**Example — NEMA 17 stepper:**
- $N_p = 50$ (50 pole pairs), $N_r = 1$ tooth per pole = effectively 50 steps/rev electrical.
- With **microstepping** (1/16 step) in DRV8825 driver:

$$
\theta_{microstep} = \dfrac{1.8°}{16} = 0.1125° = 200 \mu\text{rad}
$$

**Holding Torque** (proportional to motor current and magnetic flux):

$$
\tau_{hold} = K_{m}\,I_{a}
$$

For a NEMA 17 rated at $\tau_{hold} = 0.4$ N·m at $I = 1.5$ A:

$$
K_{m} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.5} = 0.267 \text{ N·m/A}
$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study Table for KTU Examination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison tables are very high-yield for KTU Part-B (7-mark sub-questions). Master this one.**

| Feature | Sensor | Actuator | IoT Device |
|---------|--------|----------|-----------|
| **Function** | Detect / measure | Move / control | Sense + Communicate + Actuate |
| **Direction of Energy** | Physical → Electrical | Electrical → Physical | Bi-directional (data + control) |
| **Typical Output** | V, mA, $\Omega$, Hz, digital word | Force, Torque, Displacement | Network packets (TCP/IP, MQTT) |
| **Power Requirement** | Often passive (needs excitation) | Usually active (draws large current) | Battery / mains + sleep modes |
| **Examples** | RTD, Thermocouple, LVDT, Strain Gauge | DC Motor, Solenoid, Servo, Stepper | ESP32 + DHT + Relay + Wi-Fi |
| **Standards** | IEEE 1451, IEC 60751 | IEC 60034 (rotating machines) | IEEE 802.15.4, MQTT 5.0, OPC-UA |
| **Industry 4.0 Role** | Data acquisition | Physical execution | Edge intelligence + connectivity |

---

### 3.7 Derivation: Strain Gauge Output Bridge Circuit (Wheatstone Bridge)

The most common configuration for a strain gauge uses a **Wheatstone bridge**:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex}\,\dfrac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4)}
$$

If only one arm changes by $\Delta R$ (single active gauge):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_{ex}\,\dfrac{(R + \Delta R)\,R - R\,R}{(2R + \Delta R)(2R)} \\
&\approx V_{ex}\,\dfrac{\Delta R}{4R} \quad \text{(for } \Delta R \ll R\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since gauge factor $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{out} \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon
$$

For $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $GF = 2.0$, and strain $\varepsilon = 1000\,\mu\varepsilon$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \dfrac{5}{4} \cdot 2.0 \cdot 1000 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.5 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

This low output justifies the need for **instrumentation amplifiers** (e.g., AD620, INA128) for further signal conditioning.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Industry 4.0 Perception Layer Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the functional integration of Sensors, Actuators, and IoT Devices in a typical Industry 4.0 cyber-physical production system (CPPS).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Process Variable<br>Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Humidity] --> B[Sensor Layer<br>RTD, Thermocouple, LVDT, Strain Gauge]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning<br>Amplification and Filtering]
    C --> D[ADC Conversion<br>Analog to Digital]
    D --> E[Microcontroller Unit<br>ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi]
    E --> F{Decision Logic<br>Threshold Check}
    F -- Above Threshold --> G[Communication Module<br>Wi-Fi, MQTT, LoRaWAN]
    G --> H[Cloud Server<br>AWS IoT, Azure]
    H --> I[Analytics Dashboard<br>ThingsBoard, Grafana]
    F -- Local Control --> J[Actuator Driver<br>Relay, H-Bridge, VFD]
    J --> K[Actuator Layer<br>DC Motor, Servo, Solenoid, Valve]
    K --> L[Physical Action on Process]
    L --> A
    H -- Remote Command --> J
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Smart Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Process Variable Input<br>Physical Quantity] --> S1[Primary Sensing Element<br>Resistive, Capacitive, Inductive, Piezoelectric]
    S1 --> S2[Signal Conditioning<br>Amplifier and Filter]
    S2 --> S3[ADC Converter]
    S3 --> S4[Microprocessor with Firmware<br>Calibration, Compensation, Self-Test]
    S4 --> S5[Digital Communication Interface<br>I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, Modbus]
    S5 --> N1[Network Output to PLC or Cloud]
    S4 -.-> S6[Non-Volatile Memory<br>Calibration Constants]
    S4 -.-> S7[Power Management Unit<br>Voltage Regulator]
    S7 -.-> S2
    S7 -.-> S3
    S7 -.-> S4
```

---

### 4.3 Classification Matrix: Sensors and Actuators (Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SA[Sensors and Actuators] --> SEN[Sensors]
    SA --> ACT[Actuators]

    SEN --> S1[By Signal Type]
    SEN --> S2[By Power]
    SEN --> S3[By Application]

    S1 --> S1a[Analog]
    S1 --> S1b[Digital]
    S1 --> S1c[Binary]

    S2 --> S2a[Active<br>Thermocouple, Piezo]
    S2 --> S2b[Passive<br>RTD, Strain Gauge, LDR]

    S3 --> S3a[Process<br>Pressure, Level, Flow]
    S3 --> S3b[Motion<br>Encoder, Accelerometer]
    S3 --> S3c[Vision<br>CCD, CMOS, LiDAR]

    ACT --> A1[By Energy Domain]
    A1 --> A1a[Hydraulic]
    A1 --> A1b[Pneumatic]
    A1 --> A1c[Electric]
    A1 --> A1d[Thermal]
    A1 --> A1e[Piezoelectric]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop IoT Feedback System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Reference] --> ERR[Summing Junction]
    FB[Feedback Signal] --> ERR
    ERR --> CTRL[Controller<br>PID Algorithm]
    CTRL --> CMD[Command Signal]
    CMD --> ACT[Actuator]
    ACT --> PROC[Plant or Process]
    PROC --> SENS[Sensor]
    SENS --> FB
    SENS -.-> CLOUD[Cloud Data Logger]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the loop:** The setpoint (desired value) is compared with the measured feedback from the sensor. The controller (often a PID running on an MCU) computes an error signal and commands the actuator. The actuator changes the process. The sensor measures the new state. The cycle repeats — this is **closed-loop control**, the foundation of every Industry 4.0 automation cell.
