<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Cyber-Physical Systems. List any four characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system that integrates computation, networking, and physical processes through continuous feedback loops. Embedded computers and networks monitor, coordinate, and control the physical processes via sensors and actuators in real time.

**Four key characteristics:**
1. **Integration** of cyber and physical components
2. **Real-time operation** with bounded latency
3. **Feedback loops** for closed-loop control
4. **Distributed and heterogeneous** architecture

*(Word count target: ~60 words for full marks)*

#### Question 2: Differentiate between CPS and IoT. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **CPS** | **IoT** |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Primary Focus | Control of physical process | Connectivity of devices |
| Feedback | Closed-loop (sense-compute-actuate) | Typically open-loop data flow |
| Real-time | Strict, deadline-bounded | Often best-effort |
| Computation | Embedded control algorithms | Cloud-centric data analytics |
| Goal | Act on the physical world | Sense and share data |
| Example | Autonomous braking system | Smart home temperature sensor |

**Key statement**: IoT is a *communication enabler* for CPS, but CPS adds **actuation and real-time control**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A: Explain the architecture of Cyber-Physical Systems with a neat block diagram. Discuss any two real-time industrial applications of CPS in detail.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

##### Part (a) — CPS Architecture [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A CPS consists of **three tightly integrated layers**, often called the **3C architecture**:

**1. Physical Layer** *(2 marks)*
- Contains the **physical process** (engine, reactor, vehicle) and the I/O interface
- **Sensors** capture real-world variables:
  - Temperature (RTD, thermocouple)
  - Pressure (strain gauge)
  - Position (LIDAR, encoder)
  - Flow (ultrasonic flowmeter)
- **Actuators** modify the physical state:
  - DC/stepper/servo motors
  - Pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders
  - Solenoid valves

**2. Cyber/Computation Layer** *(2 marks)*
- Embedded controllers (PLC, MCU like ARM Cortex, DSP)
- Runs **real-time operating system** (RTOS) such as FreeRTOS, VxWorks
- Executes control algorithms: **PID, MPC, fuzzy logic, neural networks**
- Performs **edge analytics** and AI inference

**3. Communication/Network Layer** *(2 marks)*
- Wired: CAN bus, Ethernet/IP, PROFINET
- Wireless: Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, LoRa, 5G
- Industrial IoT protocols: **OPC-UA, MQTT, Modbus**
- Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) for deterministic latency

**Working** *(1 mark)*: The system operates in a **closed feedback loop** — sensors measure physical state, data is communicated to the cyber layer, the controller computes an action, and the actuator modifies the physical process. The cycle repeats continuously in real time.

##### Part (b) — Two Real-Time Industrial Applications [7 Marks]

**Application 1: Autonomous Vehicle Adaptive Cruise Control** *(3.5 marks)*

A self-driving car's adaptive cruise control is a classic CPS:

- **Sensors**: Radar (front vehicle distance), LIDAR (lane detection), camera (sign recognition), wheel speed encoder
- **Cyber layer**: ECU (Electronic Control Unit) runs a **Model Predictive Control** algorithm that calculates safe following distance and target speed
- **Communication**: CAN bus links sensors, ECU, and actuators; 5G enables V2X (vehicle-to-everything) communication
- **Actuators**: Electronic throttle, brake-by-wire system, steering motor
- **Real-time constraint**: The total response time must be **$< 100\,\text{ms}$** for safe operation at highway speeds
- **Feedback loop**: Wheel speed sensor continuously reports back to ECU, which adjusts throttle/brake every $10\,\text{ms}$

**Application 2: Smart Grid Energy Management** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Sensors**: Smart meters, phasor measurement units (PMUs) at substations, weather sensors for renewable forecasting
- **Cyber layer**: SCADA system runs demand-response algorithms, load balancing, and predictive analytics
- **Communication**: Wide-area network (WAN) using fiber, 4G/5G; IEC 61850 protocol
- **Actuators**: Smart switches, capacitor banks, transformer tap changers, renewable inverter controllers
- **Real-time constraint**: Frequency regulation requires response in **$< 200\,\text{ms}$** to prevent grid instability
- **Feedback**: Voltage and frequency sensors close the loop, ensuring grid stability even with variable renewable input

---

#### **Question B: What are the design challenges in implementing Cyber-Physical Systems? Explain with reference to real-time constraints, security, and interoperability.** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`

##### Part (a) — Real-Time Constraints & Security [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Real-Time Constraints** *(2.5 marks)*

Real-time operation is the **defining design challenge** of CPS. Unlike general-purpose computing, CPS must respond within **strict deadlines** — failure to do so can cause physical harm.

- **Hard real-time**: Missing a deadline is catastrophic (aircraft autopilot, pacemakers, anti-lock brakes)
- **Soft real-time**: Missing deadlines degrades performance (smart traffic lights, video streaming)
- **Latency budget**: Total end-to-end delay must be engineered:

$$T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate} \leq T_{deadline}$$

- **Example**: A robotic arm on a production line must respond to a position error in **$< 1\,\text{ms}$** to maintain precision. Any lag causes defect products or collisions.

**Solutions**: Real-time operating systems (RTOS), time-sensitive networking, dedicated hardware accelerators, and priority-based scheduling.

**2. Security** *(2.5 marks)*

CPS are attractive targets for cyber-physical attacks that can cause **physical destruction**:

- **Stuxnet attack (2010)**: Destroyed Iranian uranium centrifuges by manipulating PLC control signals
- **Attack vectors**: Malware injection, man-in-the-middle on sensor data, false data injection, denial-of-service
- **Consequences**: Unlike pure cyber attacks, CPS attacks can cause loss of life (hacked car brakes, insulin pump overdose)

**Solutions**:
- Encryption (TLS/DTLS) for all communication
- Authentication of all sensor/actuator nodes
- Intrusion detection systems (IDS) on the network
- Sandboxing of control software
- Regular security patching and penetration testing

**3. Implementation Hurdles** *(2 marks)*

- **Heterogeneity**: Integrating sensors, controllers, and actuators from different vendors with different protocols
- **Power constraints**: Battery-powered wireless sensors must operate for years without replacement
- **Reliability**: CPS must operate 24/7 with minimal maintenance; MTBF targets exceed **$100{,}000$ hours**
- **Certification**: Safety-critical CPS (aviation, medical) require formal verification and regulatory approval (DO-178C, IEC 61508)

##### Part (b) — Interoperability & Open Issues [7 Marks]

**Interoperability** *(3.5 marks)*

CPS components come from multiple vendors using different protocols, data formats, and interfaces. Achieving seamless interoperability is a major design challenge:

- **Protocol diversity**: A factory may use Modbus (legacy), OPC-UA (modern), MQTT (IoT), and PROFINET (Siemens) simultaneously
- **Semantic interoperability**: Two systems may "speak" the same protocol but interpret data differently (e.g., temperature in Celsius vs Fahrenheit)
- **Data format heterogeneity**: JSON, XML, protobuf, binary formats must all be reconciled

**Solutions**:
- Adoption of **standardized middleware** (OPC-UA as a universal translator)
- Use of **semantic web technologies** and ontologies
- **API gateways** that translate between protocols
- Adoption of reference architectures like **RAMI 4.0** and **IIC (Industrial Internet Consortium) frameworks**

**Other Open Issues** *(3.5 marks)*

| Challenge | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Privacy** | CPS collect vast personal data (smart home, health CPS) — GDPR compliance is complex |
| **Scalability** | Adding thousands of IoT devices strains bandwidth and computation |
| **AI/ML integration** | Embedding ML models in resource-constrained edge devices |
| **Ethical concerns** | Autonomous CPS (weapons, vehicles) raise liability and ethical questions |
| **Energy efficiency** | Green CPS — reducing carbon footprint of always-on computation |
| **Verification & validation** | Formal methods to prove CPS correctness before deployment |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing marks in CPS questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CPS with IoT** — Examiners deduct marks if you use them interchangeably. Always state: *CPS uses IoT as a communication layer but adds real-time control and actuation.*
> 2. **Omitting "real-time"** — This is the **single most important keyword** in any CPS answer. Forgetting it loses 1-2 marks instantly.
> 3. **No closed-loop diagram** — Always draw the feedback loop. Even a hand-drawn arrow from actuator back to sensor wins easy marks.
> 4. **Vague application examples** — "CPS is used in factories" gets 1 mark. "CPS-controlled robotic welder in an automotive plant using PID control with $5\,\text{ms}$ response time" gets full marks. Be **specific**.
> 5. **Forgetting the cyber-physical interaction** — Don't just describe the sensors OR the algorithms. Show how they **interact** through a feedback loop.
> 6. **Missing units in numerical problems** — Always state ms, kHz, m/s, etc.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for CPS (Module 2)**

**Core Concepts:**
- CPS = **Computation + Communication + Control + Physical Process** with **closed-loop feedback**
- **Real-time** operation is the **defining feature** (bounded deadline)
- CPS uses IoT as a communication backbone but adds **actuation and control**
- Three-layer architecture: **Physical, Cyber, Communication**

**Must-Know Terms:**
- **3C Architecture** (Computation, Communication, Control)
- **Sensors and Actuators** as I/O interface
- **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**
- **OPC-UA, MQTT, CAN bus** — common CPS protocols
- **Digital Twin** — virtual replica fed by CPS data
- **RAMI 4.0** — Industry 4.0 reference architecture model

**Key Differences to Remember:**
- CPS vs IoT: **CPS acts on physical world; IoT only senses/shares data**
- CPS vs Embedded System: **CPS is networked; embedded is often standalone**
- Hard vs Soft real-time: **Hard = catastrophic failure; Soft = degraded performance**

**Critical Formulas:**
- Closed-loop transfer function: $G(s) = \frac{C(s)P(s)}{1 + C(s)P(s)H(s)}$
- End-to-end latency: $T_{e2e} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}$
- Stability criterion: All roots of $1 + C(s)P(s)H(s)$ must be in LHP
- First-order CPS time constant: $\tau' = \frac{\tau}{1 + K_p K}$

**Top Applications for KTU:**
- Autonomous vehicles (adaptive cruise control)
- Smart grid (frequency regulation)
- Smart manufacturing (robotic assembly)
- Healthcare (robotic surgery, smart prosthetics)
- Smart cities (traffic, water, buildings)

**Design Challenges (High-Yield for 14-mark questions):**
- Real-time constraints and latency budgets
- Cybersecurity and CPS attacks (Stuxnet reference)
- Interoperability across heterogeneous protocols
- Power efficiency in wireless sensor nodes
- Verification and formal safety certification

**Sample 3-Mark Definition Phrase (memorize):**
> "A Cyber-Physical System is an engineered integration of computation, networking, and physical processes, where embedded computers and sensors/actuators form a real-time closed-loop feedback system to monitor, control, and coordinate physical-world operations."

**Exam Day Reminder:**
- Always draw a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it visualizes 3 architecture
- Always include **one numerical example** — it demonstrates analytical depth
- Always mention **at least one industrial protocol** by name (OPC-UA/MQTT/CAN)
- Always close with a **real-world application** — it shows you understand the "why"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Security and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT754 - INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Security and Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**IoT Security** refers to the comprehensive set of strategies, protocols, cryptographic primitives, hardware safeguards, and software mechanisms designed to protect Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems — comprising sensors, actuators, gateways, communication networks, cloud platforms, and end-user applications — against unauthorized access, data breaches, manipulation, denial of service, and privacy violations throughout the entire device lifecycle (from manufacturing to decommissioning).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, IoT Security is treated as a **multi-layered, cross-domain discipline** spanning device-level hardware, embedded firmware, network protocols, edge/fog computing, and cloud analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** Students must be able to identify the **attack surface** of an IoT system, classify threats under the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)**, and propose countermeasures aligned with industry standards like **NIST IR 8259**, **OWASP IoT Top 10**, and **IEEE 2413**.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars: CIA Triad

IoT security is fundamentally anchored in the **CIA Triad**, which must be preserved at every layer:

| Pillar | Definition | IoT-Specific Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring data is accessible only to authorized entities | Sensor data leakage revealing personal habits |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is not altered in transit or at rest | Tampered actuator commands causing physical damage |
| **Availability** | Ensuring services remain accessible when needed | DDoS attacks shutting down smart grids |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Smart City

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "IoT as a City of a Million Doors"**
> Imagine deploying a city with **one million houses (IoT devices)**, each having a back door, a window, and a mailbox. Some houses run on **AA batteries** (constrained devices), some are built by **1,000 different manufacturers** (heterogeneity), and the postman (network) carries letters through the air visible to everyone. Now imagine a thief needs to find just **one unlocked window** to crawl in and open the gate for the entire city. **IoT Security is the discipline of locking every door, sealing every window, hiring trustworthy postmen, and watching the perimeter — simultaneously and at scale.** The challenge is that you cannot afford a heavy, expensive lock for a Rs. 50 sensor.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Standardization

- **Attack Surface**: The sum of all points (hardware, software, network, human) where an unauthorized actor can attempt to enter or extract data from the system.
- **Threat Model**: A structured representation of assets, adversaries, attack vectors, and countermeasures.
- **Vulnerability**: A weakness in a system that can be exploited to compromise security.
- **Exploit**: The actual mechanism (code, technique, or procedure) used to take advantage of a vulnerability.
- **Botnet**: A network of compromised IoT devices controlled remotely by an attacker (e.g., **Mirai Botnet** — **~145,000 devices** infected in 2016).
- **Zero-Trust Architecture**: Security model assuming no implicit trust, requiring continuous verification of every entity.

## 1.5 Why IoT Security is Fundamentally Harder than IT Security

| Factor | Traditional IT | IoT Systems |
|---|---|---|
| Device Cost | Rs. 50,000+ (PC/Server) | Rs. 50 – Rs. 5,000 (sensor) |
| Power | Mains electricity | Battery (years of life expected) |
| Compute | Multi-core GHz | MHz microcontrollers |
| Memory | GBs of RAM | KBs of RAM |
| Lifecycle | 3–5 years | 10–20 years |
| Updates | Easy (OS updates) | Often impossible after deployment |
| Heterogeneity | Limited vendors | Thousands of vendors, no standards |
| Physical Access | Restricted | Often in public/uncontrolled spaces |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IoT Security Architecture — The Four-Layer Model

IoT security is implemented as a **defense-in-depth** strategy across four layers. A breach at any single layer compromises the entire system.

### Layer 1: Perception / Device Layer (Sensors & Actuators)
- Hardware root-of-trust (TPM, secure elements)
- Tamper-resistant physical enclosures
- Hardware Unique Keys (HUK) burned at fabrication
- Secure boot via cryptographic signatures

### Layer 2: Network / Communication Layer
- Encryption in transit: **TLS 1.3**, **DTLS** (for UDP/CoAP), **IPsec**
- Mutual authentication (X.509 certificates)
- Lightweight cryptographic suites for constrained devices: **AES-128-CCM**, **ChaCha20-Poly1305**
- Network segmentation, VLANs, firewalls at the gateway

### Layer 3: Middleware / Edge-Fog-Cloud Layer
- Identity & Access Management (IAM)
- API security (OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect)
- Data-at-rest encryption (AES-256)
- Homomorphic encryption for privacy-preserving analytics
- Blockchain for tamper-evident logging

### Layer 4: Application Layer
- Secure software development lifecycle (SDLC)
- Role-based access control (RBAC)
- User behavior analytics
- Compliance with GDPR, India DPDP Act 2023

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "PNMA":** **P**erception → **N**etwork → **M**iddleware → **A**pplication. Each layer must implement Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability independently.

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of the Attack Surface

A rigorous treatment of IoT security requires quantifying the **attack surface** mathematically. For an IoT system with $n$ components, each having $v_i$ vulnerabilities and an exposure factor $e_i$:

$$A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot e_i \cdot (1 - m_i)$$

Where:
- $A_{total}$ = Total weighted attack surface
- $v_i$ = Number of known vulnerabilities in component $i$
- $e_i$ = Exposure factor (0 to 1) — proportional to network/public reachability
- $m_i$ = Mitigation effectiveness (0 to 1) — fraction of vulnerabilities already patched
- $n$ = Total number of components in the IoT ecosystem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners frequently test the relationship **$A \propto n$** — the attack surface grows **linearly with the number of devices**, making scalability the central challenge of IoT security.

## 2.3 Risk Quantification (NIST Risk Equation)

The **NIST SP 800-30** risk model adapted for IoT:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk (qualitative: Low / Medium / High)
- $T$ = Threat likelihood (probability of attack attempt, 0–1)
- $V$ = Vulnerability severity (0–1, often from CVSS scoring)
- $I$ = Impact of compromise (0–1, financial + safety + privacy)

**Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS v3.1)** score is computed as:

$$CVSS_{Base} = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$$

Where AV = Attack Vector, AC = Attack Complexity, PR = Privileges Required, UI = User Interaction, S = Scope, C = Confidentiality impact, I = Integrity impact, A = Availability impact. Scores range **0.0 – 10.0**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attack Surface: $A = \sum v_i e_i (1-m_i)$ | $v, e, m$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Quantifying exposure |
| 2 | Risk: $R = T \times V \times I$ | $T, V, I$ | Dimensionless | NIST risk model |
| 3 | Shannon Entropy (key strength): $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ | bits | Cryptographic key entropy |
| 4 | Birthday bound: $P_{coll} \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/2N}$ | $n, N$ | Dimensionless | Hash collision probability |
| 5 | Block cipher work factor | — | Operations | Brute-force resistance $2^k$ for $k$-bit key |
| 6 | Mirai infection rate | — | devices/sec | Botnet propagation speed |
| 7 | CVSS Base Score | — | 0.0 – 10.0 | Vulnerability severity |
| 8 | CIA Triad | C, I, A | Categorical | Foundational security goals |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the vertical bar `|` inside markdown tables.** For absolute value or set notation, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX.

## 2.5 Cryptographic Primitives Suited for IoT

### 2.5.1 Symmetric (Lightweight Block Ciphers)
- **AES-128**: Standard, hardware-accelerated in many MCUs
- **Speck & Simon (NSA)**: Optimized for extremely constrained devices
- **PRESENT**: Ultra-lightweight (RFID tag class)
- **ChaCha20**: Stream cipher, software-friendly

### 2.5.2 Asymmetric (for Key Exchange & Signatures)
- **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)**: 256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA security
- **Ed25519**: Modern signature scheme, fast verification
- **X25519**: Key exchange, used in TLS 1.3
- **RSA-2048+**: Avoid in resource-constrained devices if possible

### 2.5.3 Hashing
- **SHA-256**: Industry standard
- **SHA-3 (Keccak)**: Different internal structure (sponge construction)
- **BLAKE2**: Faster than SHA-256, secure

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Industry 4.0 deployments (smart factories, predictive maintenance, digital twins), IoT security directly impacts:
1. **Worker safety** — tampered actuator commands can cause physical harm.
2. **Production continuity** — DDoS attacks can halt entire assembly lines.
3. **Intellectual property** — stolen process data erodes competitive advantage.
4. **Regulatory compliance** — failures trigger penalties under DPDP Act 2023, EU GDPR.
5. **Insurance liability** — cyber-insurance premiums scale with security posture.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Attack Surface Grows with Network Scale

Consider an IoT deployment with $n$ devices. Assume each device exposes an average of $k$ network-accessible services (e.g., HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, Telnet). The total exposed service endpoints $E$ is:

$$E = n \cdot k$$

If we assume each endpoint has an independent probability $p$ of containing at least one unpatched vulnerability, the probability that **at least one** endpoint across the entire deployment is vulnerable is given by the complement of the probability that **none** are vulnerable:

$$P(\text{at least one vulnerable}) = 1 - (1 - p)^E$$

Substituting $E = n \cdot k$:

$$P(\text{breach}) = 1 - (1 - p)^{n \cdot k}$$

**Step-by-step expansion for small $n$** using the binomial identity $(1-p)^E \approx e^{-pE}$ when $pE$ is small:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{breach}) &= 1 - e^{-p \cdot n \cdot k} \\
&= 1 - e^{-pkn}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Even if $p$ is very small (e.g., $p = 0.001$, meaning 1 in 1000 endpoints vulnerable), as $n$ grows from 100 to 100,000 devices:

- For $n = 100$: $P = 1 - e^{-0.1} \approx 0.0952$ (≈ **9.5%**)
- For $n = 10{,}000$: $P = 1 - e^{-10} \approx 0.99995$ (≈ **99.995%**)
- For $n = 100{,}000$: $P = 1 - e^{-100} \approx 1.0$ (≈ **100%**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This derivation mathematically proves why **security must scale sub-linearly** with device count. KTU examiners love this result because it directly justifies the architectural shift toward **edge gateways, micro-segmentation, and zero-trust models** in large-scale IoT.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cryptographic Key Strength (Shannon Entropy)

A cryptographic key with $N$ possible equally-likely values has entropy:

$$H = \log_2(N) \text{ bits}$$

For a 128-bit key with uniform distribution ($N = 2^{128}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \log_2(2^{128}) \\
&= 128 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Work factor for brute-force** = $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ operations. At **$10^{18}$ operations/sec** (modern supercomputer), time to break = $3.4 \times 10^{20}$ seconds $\approx 10^{13}$ years — **physically infeasible**.

For IoT, a 128-bit key requires 16 bytes of storage and is **universally supported** in modern MCUs (AES-128 hardware acceleration is standard in ARM Cortex-M, ESP32, RPi Pico).

## 3.3 Derivation: Risk Score Example (KTU Numerical-Style Question)

**Problem:** A smart water treatment plant has 500 IoT sensors. Each sensor exposes 2 services. Historical data shows each service has $p = 0.002$ probability of a known unpatched vulnerability. The plant estimates that:
- Threat likelihood: $T = 0.3$
- Average vulnerability severity: $V = 0.7$
- Impact if compromised: $I = 0.9$ (water contamination = public health emergency)

**Step 1 — Calculate exposure endpoints:**
$$E = n \cdot k = 500 \times 2 = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate breach probability:**
$$P(\text{breach}) = 1 - (1 - 0.002)^{1000}$$
$$= 1 - (0.998)^{1000}$$
$$\approx 1 - e^{-2} \quad \text{(using } (1-p)^E \approx e^{-pE} \text{ for small } pE\text{)}$$
$$= 1 - 0.1353$$
$$\approx 0.8647$$

**Step 3 — Apply NIST risk equation** (using breach probability as effective threat likelihood):
$$R = T' \times V \times I = 0.8647 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 \approx 0.5448$$

**Step 4 — Classify risk** (typical scale: <0.33 Low, 0.33–0.66 Medium, >0.66 High):
$$R \approx 0.54 \implies \textbf{Medium-to-High Risk}$$

**Recommendation:** Deploy **micro-segmentation** and **hardware security modules (HSMs)** to reduce $p$ to $< 0.0005$, bringing $P(\text{breach})$ below 0.4.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: IoT Threat Classification

| Threat Category | Specific Attack | CIA Pillar Violated | Affected Layer | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical | Node tampering, side-channel | Integrity, Confidentiality | Perception | Tamper-proof enclosures, HSM |
| Network | Eavesdropping, MITM | Confidentiality | Network | TLS 1.3, DTLS |
| Network | Replay attack | Integrity, Authenticity | Network | Nonces, timestamps |
| Software | Buffer overflow, RCE | All three | Middleware | Memory-safe languages, ASLR |
| Software | Firmware modification | Integrity | Perception | Signed firmware, secure boot |
| System | DDoS / Botnet (Mirai) | Availability | All | Rate limiting, anomaly detection |
| System | Sybil attack | Authenticity | Network | Identity verification, PKI |
| Privacy | Inference attack | Confidentiality | Application | Differential privacy, homomorphic enc. |
| Cryptographic | Quantum decryption | Confidentiality | All | Post-quantum crypto (Kyber, Dilithium) |

## 3.5 Detailed Case Study: Mirai Botnet (2016)

**Background:** Mirai infected **~145,000 IoT devices** (CCTV cameras, DVRs, home routers) by exploiting **default credentials**.

**Attack mechanics (step-by-step):**
1. Scanner module sends SYN packets to random IPs on **port 23 (Telnet)** and **port 2323**.
2. On connection, brute-forces using a **61-entry dictionary** of default factory usernames/passwords (e.g., admin/admin, root/xc3511, support/blink182).
3. Successful login → downloads Mirai binary via wget/TFTP.
4. Infected device reports to **Command & Control (C2) server** via IRC.
5. C2 issues orders to launch **SYN flood, UDP flood, HTTP GET flood** against targets.
6. Peak attack: **~1.1 Tbps** against Dyn DNS (October 21, 2016) — took down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, GitHub for several hours.

**Counter-lessons for KTU exam:**
- **Lesson 1:** Default credentials must be **forced change** at first boot.
- **Lesson 2:** Disable unnecessary services (Telnet port 23 should be closed by default).
- **Lesson 3:** Network-level rate limiting can halt infection propagation.
- **Lesson 4:** Devices need **cryptographic identity**, not just username/password.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse Mirai with traditional DDoS botnets. Remember: **Mirai's innovation is using IoT-specific default credentials as the attack vector**, not the DDoS technique itself.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
