# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define CNC. List any four advantages of CNC over conventional machining. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Model Answer:**

> **CNC (Computer Numerical Control)** is a microprocessor-based manufacturing system in which a stored part-program controls the motion of machine tools and auxiliary functions automatically, in accordance with ISO 6983 (G-code/M-code) standards.

**Four Advantages of CNC:**
1. **Higher accuracy and repeatability** (±0.0025 mm to ±0.01 mm achievable).
2. **Higher productivity** — 24/7 unmanned operation possible.
3. **Greater flexibility** — change of part requires only program change, not retooling.
4. **Lower scrap rate** — once verified, the program produces identical parts.

---

### Question 2: What is SCADA? List its four main components. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Model Answer:**

> **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized, computer-based industrial automation system that monitors, gathers, and processes real-time data from geographically distributed field devices to control equipment and processes from a remote control station.

**Four Main Components:**
1. **Sensors and Actuators (Field Devices)**
2. **RTUs / PLCs (Control Layer)**
3. **SCADA Server with HMI (Supervisory Layer)**
4. **Communication Infrastructure (Network + Protocols)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Module 4 — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a CNC machine. Discuss the functions of the Machine Control Unit (MCU). (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** What is SCADA? Explain its four-level architecture with a suitable diagram and list any four industrial applications. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A (a):

**Architecture of a CNC Machine:**

A CNC system consists of **three major subsystems**:

1. **Part Program (Software)** — Contains the sequence of instructions in standard G-code (e.g., G01 linear interpolation, M03 spindle ON) and M-code.

2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** — The microprocessor-based brain that:
   - Decodes the part program block-by-block.
   - Performs **interpolation** (linear, circular, helical) to generate smooth tool paths.
   - Feeds **pulse commands** to servo drives.
   - Monitors **feedback signals** from encoders.
   - Executes **auxiliary functions** (coolant, tool change).
   - Maintains **real-time error correction** via PID logic.

3. **Machine Tool (Mechanical)** — Includes spindle, slides, ball screws, ATC (Automatic Tool Changer), and workpiece holding fixtures. The servo motors physically drive the slides along X, Y, Z axes.

**Block Diagram (as drawn in Section 4.1):**

$$\text{Part Program} \rightarrow \text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{Servo Drive} \rightarrow \text{Servo Motor} \rightarrow \text{Machine Tool}$$

with **feedback loop**: Machine Tool → Encoder → MCU (closed-loop correction).

**Block Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid architecture.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A (b):

**SCADA Definition:** (as in Section 1.3)

**Four-Level Architecture:**

| Level | Layer | Function |
|:-----:|:------|:---------|
| **0** | Field Devices | Sensors, actuators, motors, valves. |
| **1** | Control | PLCs, RTUs — local automation logic. |
| **2** | Supervisory | SCADA server, HMI, alarm engine, historian. |
| **3** | Management | MES, ERP, cloud analytics, AI engines. |

**Four Industrial Applications of SCADA:**
1. **Electric power generation, transmission, and distribution** (National Grid control).
2. **Water and wastewater treatment plants**.
3. **Oil and gas** (pipeline monitoring, refinery control).
4. **Manufacturing plant-wide OEE monitoring and predictive maintenance**.

**Block Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the Mermaid architecture.

---

### **Module 4 — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of a closed-loop CNC machine. Derive the formula for spindle speed and material removal rate in CNC milling. (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** Compare CNC and SCADA systems in terms of function, architecture, real-time criticality, and applications. Discuss how they integrate in a modern Industry 4.0 smart factory. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B (a):

**Closed-Loop CNC Working Principle:**

In a **closed-loop CNC**, the actual position of the tool/worktable is continuously measured by **encoders (rotary) or linear scales (linear)** and fed back to the controller. The MCU computes the **error signal**:

$$e(t) = X_{cmd}(t) - X_{actual}(t)$$

A **PID controller** then generates a corrective signal:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This drives the servo motor to **minimize the error** to near zero, ensuring high accuracy.

**Derivation of Spindle Speed (N):** (as in Section 3.1)

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

**Derivation of MRR:** (as in Section 3.2)

$$MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** (Refer to Section 3.4 for full calculation.)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B (b):

**Comparison CNC vs. SCADA:** (Refer to Section 2.3 comparison matrix.)

**Integration in Industry 4.0 Smart Factory:**

- **CNC machines** act as **smart, connected assets** that publish real-time data (spindle load, tool wear, vibration, energy consumption) via **OPC UA** and **MQTT** protocols.
- **SCADA** aggregates data from all CNCs, PLCs, and robots into a **central historian database**.
- The historian feeds **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** and **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)** systems.
- **Cloud-based AI/ML engines** analyze the data for **predictive maintenance, quality prediction, and energy optimization**.
- The AI insights are sent back to the SCADA HMI as **alerts and recommendations**, closing the cyber-physical loop.

**Integration Block Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating SCADA with PLC:** SCADA does **not** directly control field devices — it **supervises** PLCs and RTUs. Writing "SCADA controls the motor" is a **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the feedback loop in CNC:** A CNC block diagram **without the feedback arrow from encoder to MCU** is incomplete. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for an open-loop diagram when asked for closed-loop.
>
> 3. **Wrong units in numerical problems:** Mixing RPM with rad/s, or mm³/min with cm³/min, leads to **0.5–1 mark deduction**. Always write the unit alongside the number.
>
> 4. **Vague definitions:** Writing "CNC is automation in manufacturing" is **worth 0 marks**. Use the formal definition with **"microprocessor-based," "part-program," "ISO 6983,"** and **"real-time motion control."**
>
> 5. **Ignoring Industry 4.0 keywords:** In any automation answer, you **must** mention **IIoT, OPC UA, MQTT, Digital Twin, or Cyber-Physical System** to score full marks in the 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CNC & SCADA Systems**

### CNC Essentials
- ☐ CNC = **C**omputer **N**umerical **C**ontrol — microprocessor-controlled NC machine.
- ☐ Three subsystems: **Part Program → MCU → Machine Tool**.
- ☐ Two standards: **ISO 6983 (G-code)** and **ISO 14649 (STEP-NC)**.
- ☐ Open-loop = stepper motors; **Closed-loop = servo motors + encoders**.
- ☐ G-codes (geometry: G00, G01, G02) and M-codes (miscellaneous: M03, M05, M08).
- ☐ Axes: **X, Y, Z** (linear); **A, B, C** (rotary).
- ☐ **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ RPM.
- ☐ **MRR formula:** $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ mm³/min.
- ☐ **Feed rate formula:** $F = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z$ mm/min.
- ☐ **PID control** equation: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$.
- ☐ Repeatability: **±0.0025 mm to ±0.01 mm**; high-end CNC: **< 1 μm**.

### SCADA Essentials
- ☐ SCADA = **S**upervisory **C**ontrol **a**nd **D**ata **A**cquisition.
- ☐ SCADA is **not** a direct controller — it **supervises** PLCs/RTUs.
- ☐ Four levels: **Field → Control → Supervisory → Management**.
- ☐ Components: **Sensors, RTUs, PLCs, SCADA Server, HMI, Historian, Communication Network**.
- ☐ Common protocols: **Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, OPC UA, Profinet, EtherNet/IP**.
- ☐ Applications: **Power grid, water treatment, oil & gas, manufacturing plant monitoring**.
- ☐ **Soft real-time** (seconds-level), unlike CNC's **hard real-time** (microsecond-level).
- ☐ Key KPI from SCADA data: **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**.

### Industry 4.0 Integration
- ☐ CNC + SCADA = **Cyber-Physical Production System (CPPS)**.
- ☐ Communication glue: **OPC UA, MQTT, REST APIs**.
- ☐ Cloud layer: **AI/ML for predictive maintenance, Digital Twin, Big Data analytics**.
- ☐ OEE and historian data feed **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** and **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics for KTU Viva & ESE:**
> - **CNC = "Code → Controller → Cutting"** (Part Program → MCU → Machine Tool).
> - **SCADA = "Sensors Call Data; Alarms Disturb All"** (Sensors → Communication → Data → Alarms).
> - **Levels of SCADA = "F-C-S-M"** (Field → Control → Supervisory → Management).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Trends: Digital Twins, Augmented Reality, and Blockchain in Industry 4.0
Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT754 - INDUSTRY 4.0 > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications # Module 4 — Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications (ELSI) of Industry 4.0 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry 4.0 Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications (ELSI)** refer to the multidimensional set of concerns, frameworks, and consequences arising from the integration of cyber-physical systems (CPS), Internet of Things (IoT), Artificial Intelligence (AI), big data analytics, and additive manufacturing into industrial and societal workflows. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** outcome-based framework, ELSI is defined as the systematic study of: - The **moral duties** (Ethics) of engineers and organizations deploying autonomous systems, - The **regulatory and legislative** (Legal) mechanisms governing data ownership, liability, intellectual property, and worker rights, and - The **broader human and societal consequences** (Social) such as workforce displacement, the digital divide, surveillance, and inequality. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Driverless Car Society":** > Imagine a fully autonomous car navigating city streets. **Ethically**, who decides whether the car prioritizes the passenger's life or a pedestrian's life in an unavoidable crash (the Trolley Problem)? **Legally**, if the car causes an accident, is the manufacturer, software vendor, or owner liable? **Socially**, millions of taxi, truck, and delivery drivers may lose their livelihoods. This single example captures the three pillars of ELSI — showing that every Industry 4.0 innovation is not just a technological change, but a **socio-technical transformation**. ### 1.3 The Three Pillars of ELSI — At a Glance | Pillar | Core Focus | Key Question | KTU 2024 CO Mapping | |---|---|---|---| | **Ethical** | Moral responsibility of designers, deployers, and AI systems | "Is it the right thing to do?" | CO4 (Understand societal context) | | **Legal** | Laws, regulations, IP, contracts, liability | "Is it permissible by law?" | CO4 (Compliance awareness) | | **Social** | Human impact, labor, equity, culture | "Who is affected and how?" | CO4 (Sustainable engineering) | ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Benchmarks The following **measurable metrics** are mandated by KTU for ELSI assessment in Industry 4.0 projects: - **Automation Risk Index (ARI)** — Probability score of a job being automated (scale: **0 to 1**, where **1 = full automation likely**). - **Gini Coefficient for Digital Wealth** — Measures income inequality from digital economy (**0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality**). - **GDPR Compliance Score** — Adherence to **EU General Data Protection Regulation 2016/679**. - **Cyber-Physical Safety Standard** — **ISO/IEC 27001** and **IEC 62443** compliance for industrial systems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Ethical Implications — Theoretical Framework ### 2.1.1 The Five Core Ethical Pillars in Industry 4.0 The ethical dimension of Industry 4.0 revolves around five foundational principles, drawn from classical engineering ethics (PMI Code of Ethics, IEEE Code of Ethics, ACM Code): 1. **Transparency & Explainability** — AI and ML models must be interpretable (XAI — Explainable AI). 2. **Accountability** — Clear lines of responsibility for autonomous decisions. 3. **Fairness & Non-Discrimination** — Algorithms must not exhibit bias (gender, racial, economic). 4. **Privacy & Data Sovereignty** — Respecting personal and organizational data ownership. 5. **Human-Centricity** — Technology serves humans, not the reverse (Human-in-the-Loop principle). ### 2.1.2 The Ethical Decision Framework (KTU Recommended Model) When a KTU engineer encounters an ethical dilemma in an Industry 4.0 context, the following four-step decision matrix is recommended: - **Step 1 — Identify Stakeholders:** Who is affected? (Workers, customers, society, environment) - **Step 2 — Apply Deontological Test:** Is the action intrinsically right per professional codes? (Duty-based) - **Step 3 — Apply Consequentialist Test:** Does it maximize overall welfare? (Outcome-based) - **Step 4 — Virtue Ethics Check:** Would a virtuous engineer with practical wisdom (*phronesis*) approve? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **outcome-based education (OBE)**. Therefore, ethical analysis questions expect students to demonstrate **CO4 (Understand the impact of engineering solutions in societal and environmental contexts)** by applying multi-framework reasoning, not just stating one principle. ## 2.2 Legal Implications — Theoretical Framework ### 2.2.1 Key Legal Domains Affected by Industry 4.0 - **Data Protection Law:** GDPR (EU), CCPA (California), India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**. - **Intellectual Property (IP) Law:** Patents for AI-generated inventions, copyright of training data, trade secrets in digital twin models. - **Liability Law:** Product liability, vicarious liability for autonomous systems. - **Labor Law:** Gig economy classification, right to disconnect, algorithmic management. - **Cybersecurity Law:** Mandatory breach disclosure (e.g., **72-hour notification under GDPR Article 33**). ### 2.2.2 The Liability Assignment Matrix (LAM) In Industry 4.0, when a cyber-physical system causes harm, liability flows through a chain: $$\text{Manufacturer} \rightarrow \text{System Integrator} \rightarrow \text{Software Vendor} \rightarrow \text{Operator} \rightarrow \text{End User}$$ The challenge in Industry 4.0 is that **autonomous decision-making** by AI breaks this linear chain. Legal scholars propose: - The **"Black Box Problem"** — Difficulty in explaining AI decisions legally. - The **"Distributed Responsibility Model"** — Shared liability across the CPS value chain. - The **"Strict Liability"** doctrine — Manufacturer held liable regardless of fault (common in product liability). ## 2.3 Social Implications — Theoretical Framework ### 2.3.1 The Social Trilemma of Industry 4.0 > [!NOTE] > **The Social Trilemma:** Industry 4.0 simultaneously promises three desirable outcomes that are often mutually conflicting: > 1. **Productivity Growth** (economic gains from automation) > 2. **Job Preservation** (sustaining employment levels) > 3. **Inclusive Development** (equitable distribution of benefits) > > Optimizing two typically compromises the third. ### 2.3.2 Workforce Transformation Model (Frey & Osborne, 2017 Style) The probability of computerization for a given job is given by: $$P(\text{Automation}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \sigma_i$$ Where: - $P(\text{Automation})$ = Probability of automation (**dimensionless**, range **0 to 1**) - $w_i$ = Weight of task $i$ in the occupation (**dimensionless**, $\sum w_i = 1$) - $\sigma_i$ = Automation potential of task $i$ (**dimensionless**, range **0 to 1**) - $n$ = Total number of occupational tasks If $P(\text{Automation}) \geq 0.7$, the job is classified as **"High Risk"** for automation. ### 2.3.3 The Digital Divide Index (DDI) The digital divide measures unequal access to Industry 4.0 benefits: $$\text{DDI} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( \frac{A_j^{\max} - A_j^{\text{actual}}}{A_j^{\max}} \right)}{m}$$ Where: - $A_j^{\max}$ = Maximum access to digital resource $j$ (**number of users, dimensionless**) - $A_j^{\text{actual}}$ = Actual access in given population (**number of users, dimensionless**) - $m$ = Number of digital resources measured (**dimensionless**) A **DDI approaching 1** indicates severe digital exclusion. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU ESE 2024 — Save This for Revision!** | # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Units / Range | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Automation Risk | $P(\text{Auto}) = \sum w_i \cdot \sigma_i$ | Dimensionless, **0 to 1** | Workforce planning, reskilling budgets | | 2 | Digital Divide | $\text{DDI} = \frac{1}{m}\sum \frac{A_j^{\max} - A_j^{\text{actual}}}{A_j^{\max}}$ | Dimensionless, **0 to 1** | Policy design, digital inclusion programs | | 3 | GDPR Breach Window | $t_{\text{notify}} \leq 72$ hours | Hours | Cybersecurity SLA design | | 4 | Bias Fairness Index | $\Delta_{\text{bias}} = \vert P(Y=1 \mid S=0) - P(Y=1 \mid S=1) \vert$ | Dimensionless, **0 to 1** | Algorithmic auditing | | 5 | Liability Allocation | $L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k} \lambda_k \cdot L_k$ | Monetary units (₹, $) | Insurance and contract design | | 6 | Network Exposure Risk | $\text{NER} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ | Dimensionless, **0 to 1** | Cybersecurity risk scoring | | 7 | Skill Obsolescence Rate | $r_{\text{skill}} = \frac{S_{\text{obsolete}}}{S_{\text{total}}}$ | Dimensionless, **0 to 1** | Curriculum redesign for universities | **Note on notation:** $\lambda_k$ is the liability share of stakeholder $k$; $p$ is per-device breach probability; $n$ is number of connected IoT nodes; $S$ represents sensitive attribute in fairness metrics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Quantifying the Automation Risk for a CNC Operator Role > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context (KTU Board Exam Pattern):** A factory in Kerala is automating its CNC machining cell. The CNC operator role consists of **4 main tasks**: machine setup ($T_1$), quality inspection ($T_2$), tool change ($T_3$), and routine maintenance ($T_4$). Compute the automation probability and classify the job risk. **Given Data:** | Task | Weight ($w_i$) | Automation Potential ($\sigma_i$) | |---|---|---| | $T_1$: Machine setup | **0.30** | **0.85** | | $T_2$: Quality inspection | **0.25** | **0.90** | | $T_3$: Tool change | **0.20** | **0.95** | | $T_4$: Routine maintenance | **0.25** | **0.40** | **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{Automation}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot \sigma_i \\ &= (w_1 \cdot \sigma_1) + (w_2 \cdot \sigma_2) + (w_3 \cdot \sigma_3) + (w_4 \cdot \sigma_4) \\ &= (0.30 \times 0.85) + (0.25 \times 0.90) + (0.20 \times 0.95) + (0.25 \times 0.40) \\ &= 0.255 + 0.225 + 0.190 + 0.100 \\ &= 0.770 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Since $P(\text{Automation}) = 0.770 \geq 0.7$, the CNC operator role is classified as **HIGH RISK** for automation. The factory should plan for **reskilling programs** focusing on the maintenance task $T_4$ (lowest automation potential, $\sigma_4 = 0.40$), as it represents the **human-value residual**. **Engineering Decision:** Transition strategy should emphasize **human-machine collaboration (HMC)** rather than full replacement, preserving the $T_4$ function as a human supervisory role. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bias Detection in an AI Hiring System > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A KTU B.Tech project involves an AI-based resume screening system. The fairness audit reveals: $P(\text{Hire} \mid \text{Gender} = \text{Female}) = 0.32$ and $P(\text{Hire} \mid \text{Gender} = \text{Male}) = 0.48$. Compute the bias index. $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_{\text{bias}} &= \vert P(Y=1 \mid S=0) - P(Y=1 \mid S=1) \vert \\ &= \vert 0.32 - 0.48 \vert \\ &= 0.16 \end{aligned} $$ Where $Y=1$ denotes hiring decision and $S$ is the sensitive attribute (gender). **Interpretation:** A bias index of **0.16** is considered **moderate bias**. For KTU ethical compliance (mapped to **CO4**), the team must apply **re-weighting** or **adversarial debiasing** techniques until $\Delta_{\text{bias}} \leq 0.05$. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Network Exposure Risk in a Smart Factory A smart factory has **n = 50** IoT sensors. Each sensor has a breach probability of **p = 0.02**. Compute the probability that **at least one** sensor is compromised in a given year. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NER} &= 1 - (1-p)^{n} \\ &= 1 - (1 - 0.02)^{50} \\ &= 1 - (0.98)^{50} \end{aligned} $$ Now computing $(0.98)^{50}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln(0.98) &= -0.02020 \\ 50 \times \ln(0.98) &= -1.010 \\ (0.98)^{50} &= e^{-1.010} \approx 0.3642 \\ \text{NER} &= 1 - 0.3642 = 0.6358 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** There is a **63.58% probability** that at least one IoT sensor is breached annually. This justifies investing in **IEC 62443** compliant segmentation and **zero-trust architecture** for the factory network. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ELSI Trade-off Analysis for Industry 4.0 Adoption | Stakeholder | Ethical Concern | Legal Concern | Social Concern | Mitigation Strategy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Factory Worker** | Surveillance & dignity | Right to privacy | Job loss & reskilling needs | Human-in-the-loop, transparent HR analytics | | **Consumer** | Algorithmic fairness | Product liability | Price discrimination | GDPR compliance, audit trails | | **Manufacturer** | IP protection vs. openness | Patent litigation | Reputation risk | Open-source governance, ESG reporting | | **Government** | Digital sovereignty | Regulatory gaps | Inequality, unemployment | National AI strategies, Universal Basic Income pilots | | **Environment** | E-waste from sensors | E-waste disposal law | Resource depletion | Circular economy, green manufacturing | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The ELSI Triangle — Conceptual Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the interrelationship between Ethical, Legal, and Social dimensions in Industry 4.0 decision-making, along with the central role of the **KTU Engineer**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Industry 4.0 Technology
AI IoT CPS BigData] --> B[Ethical Dimension
Moral Responsibility] A --> C[Legal Dimension
Regulatory Compliance] A --> D[Social Dimension
Human Impact] B --> E[Transparency Fairness Accountability] C --> F[GDPR IP Law Liability Labor] D --> G[Employment Equity Digital Divide] E --> H[KTU Engineer Decision Center] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Sustainable Industry 4.0
Outcome CO4] B -.Feedback.-> C C -.Feedback.-> D D -.Feedback.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ELSI Governance in Smart Manufacturing ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT_LAYER[Input Layer] I1[Sensor Data Streams] I2[Worker Biometric Data] I3[Customer Personal Data] end subgraph PROCESS_LAYER[Processing Layer] P1[AI ML Models] P2[Digital Twin Engine] P3[Decision Support System] end subgraph ELSI_LAYER[ELSI Governance Layer] E1[Ethics Review Board] L1[Legal Compliance Module] S1[Social Impact Assessment] end subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER[Output Layer] O1[Auditable Decisions] O2[Compliance Reports] O3[Stakeholder Dashboards] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P2 I3 --> P3 P1 --> E1 P2 --> L1 P3 --> S1 E1 --> O1 L1 --> O2 S1 --> O3 O1 --> FEED[Feedback to KTU Engineer] O2 --> FEED O3 --> FEED ``` ## 4.3 Liability Flow Diagram — Autonomous System Failure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Failure Event in CPS] --> B{Is Cause Identifiable?} B -->|Yes| C[Manufacturer Liability] B -->|No| D[Distributed Responsibility Model] C --> E[Insurance Claim Settlement] D --> F1[Software Vendor Share] D --> F2[Operator Share] D --> F3[End User Share] F1 --> G[Court Arbitration] F2 --> G F3 --> G E --> H[Resolution] G --> H ``` ## 4.4 KTU Course Outcome (CO4) Mapping Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD CO4[CO4: Understand Societal and Environmental Context] CO4 --> C1[Ethical Awareness] CO4 --> C2[Legal Literacy] CO4 --> C3[Social Responsibility] C1 --> A1[Case Study Analysis] C2 --> A2[GDPR Compliance Exercise] C3 --> A3[Stakeholder Impact Report] A1 --> PO[Program Outcome PO12: Life-long Learning] A2 --> PO A3 --> PO ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Digital Divide" as it applies to Industry 4.0. List two consequences.** **Model Answer:** The **Digital Divide** refers to the gap between populations that have ready access to Industry 4.0 technologies (such as broadband internet, IoT devices, AI tools, and digital literacy) and those that do not. It is quantitatively measured by the **Digital Divide Index (DDI)**, ranging from **0 (no divide)** to **1 (maximum divide)**. **Two consequences are:** 1. **Economic Inequality** — Communities without digital access cannot participate in the digital economy, exacerbating poverty. 2. **Skill Obsolescence** — Workers without access to digital upskilling platforms become unemployable in Industry 4.0 environments. *(Maps to CO4 — 3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for consequences)* ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **What is the "Right to be Forgotten" under GDPR? Why is it relevant to Industry 4.0?** **Model Answer:** The **Right to be Forgotten**, codified under **Article 17 of the GDPR**, grants individuals the right to request that personal data be erased by data controllers without undue delay. **Relevance to Industry 4.0:** Smart factories, IoT devices, and digital twins continuously collect massive personal and behavioral data. Without mechanisms to honor erasure requests, organizations face legal penalties of up to **€20 million or 4% of annual global turnover**, whichever is higher. *(Maps to CO4 — 3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for relevance)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT** write generic essays on "ethics in engineering." KTU examiners expect **Industry 4.0-specific** examples (AI bias, IoT surveillance, digital twin data, etc.). > - **Always** cite a specific law, regulation, or framework (GDPR, DPDPA 2023, IEEE Ethics Code). > - For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: **(a) Theory [7 marks] + (b) Application/Case Study [7 marks]**. > - **Common deduction:** Failure to map answers to CO4 (societal/environmental context) results in 1-2 mark deduction per sub-question. --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Ethical Implications of Artificial Intelligence in Industry 4.0**. Discuss at least four ethical principles with Industry 4.0-specific examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following case study: > *"A Kerala-based automotive parts manufacturer has deployed an AI-powered recruitment tool to screen resumes. After 6 months, internal audits reveal that the tool systematically rejects 70% of female applicants for technical roles, despite equivalent qualifications. The HR head argues that the algorithm is 'objective' and 'data-driven'."* Analyze this case using the **Ethical Decision Framework** discussed in your syllabus. What legal provisions under the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023** and the **Indian Constitution (Article 14/15/16)** are relevant? Recommend three corrective actions. **Model Answer:** **Part (a) — Ethical Implications of AI in Industry 4.0:** AI is the cognitive backbone of Industry 4.0, embedded in predictive maintenance, quality control, supply chain optimization, and human-robot collaboration. Four ethical principles are: 1. **Transparency & Explainability** — Engineers must deploy *Explainable AI (XAI)* techniques (e.g., SHAP, LIME) so that stakeholders understand why an AI made a decision. *Example:* A predictive maintenance system flagging a turbine should provide interpretable reasons (vibration anomaly, temperature spike) rather than a black-box alert. 2. **Fairness & Non-Discrimination** — Training data must be audited for historical biases. *Example:* Resume screening AI trained on past (male-dominated) hiring data perpetuates gender bias — as illustrated in Part (b). 3. **Accountability** — When an autonomous robot causes workplace injury, clear accountability must be established. The IEEE Global Initiative on Ethics of Autonomous and Intelligent Systems recommends **"Meaningful Human Control"** as a safeguard. 4. **Privacy by Design** — AI systems processing worker biometrics, location, and performance data must embed privacy from the design stage, aligned to **GDPR Article 25**. **Part (b) — Case Study Analysis:** **Ethical Analysis (using the four-step framework):** - **Step 1 — Stakeholders:** Female applicants, the employer, society at large. - **Step 2 — Deontological Test:** Discrimination violates the duty of equal treatment (violates IEEE Code of Ethics #1). - **Step 3 — Consequentialist Test:** The outcome perpetuates systemic gender inequality — fails the welfare test. - **Step 4 — Virtue Ethics:** A virtuous engineer would recognize this as unjust and demand rectification. **Legal Provisions:** - **DPDPA 2023, Section 8:** Prohibits processing of personal data that causes harm or is biased. - **Article 14 of Indian Constitution:** Right to equality before law. - **Article 15:** Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of sex. - **Article 16:** Equality of opportunity in public employment. **Three Corrective Actions:** 1. **Re-train the model** on balanced, gender-audited datasets. 2. **Implement bias auditing** quarterly using fairness metrics (e.g., $\Delta_{\text{bias}} \leq 0.05$). 3. **Establish a Human Review Board** for all AI-screened rejections, ensuring human oversight of automated decisions. *(Marks distribution: 7 marks = 3 for (a) explanation + 4 for (b) structured case analysis with legal references)* --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]` **(Alternative Choice)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Legal Implications of Industry 4.0** with special reference to **(i)** Data Protection laws and **(ii)** Intellectual Property rights in AI-generated innovations. **(b) [7 Marks]** A semiconductor company uses **digital twin technology** to simulate production processes. The digital twin model is developed jointly by a vendor and an in-house team. A competitor reverse-engineers the model. **Discuss the IP and liability issues involved.** Reference the **Indian Patents Act 1970**, **Copyright Act 1957**, and relevant case law principles. **Model Answer:** **Part (a) — Legal Implications:** **(i) Data Protection Laws:** Industry 4.0 relies on massive data collection from IoT sensors, cameras, and connected devices. Key legal frameworks include: - **GDPR (EU, 2016/679):** Mandates lawful basis for processing, data minimization, and 72-hour breach notification. - **DPDPA (India, 2023):** India's first comprehensive data protection law, establishing the *Data Protection Board of India* for grievance redressal. - **CCPA (California, 2018):** Grants consumers rights to know, delete, and opt out of data sales. **(ii) Intellectual Property in AI:** - **Patent Issues:** Under the Indian Patents Act 1970, **Section 3(k)** excludes "computer programs per se" from patentability. However, AI-enabled industrial processes may be patentable if they produce a technical effect. - **Copyright:** Training data used in AI models raises complex copyright questions — the **Authors vs. Anthropic (2023)** lawsuit in the US exemplifies this. - **Trade Secrets:** Digital twin models, if kept confidential with reasonable security measures, qualify as trade secrets under the **Indian Contract Act**. **Part (b) — IP and Liability Case:** The digital twin model is a **joint work** under Section 2(z) of the Copyright Act 1957, with shared authorship between the vendor and in-house team. The reverse-engineering by the competitor raises: 1. **Copyright Infringement:** Reverse-engineering for interoperability is permitted under certain jurisdictions, but **clean-room reverse engineering** is required to avoid direct copying of expressive elements. 2. **Trade Secret Misappropriation:** If the model was never publicly disclosed and was acquired through improper means, the **NDAs** and **Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act** provide remedies. 3. **Patent Considerations:** If the model incorporates a patented process (e.g., a novel manufacturing simulation algorithm), the competitor may infringe under **Section 104 of the Patents Act**. 4. **Liability:** The original developers can claim **damages, injunction, and account of profits** under **Order VII Rule 1 of CPC** read with **Specific Relief Act 1963**. **Recommendations:** File for patent protection of the underlying algorithm, register copyright for the digital twin model, and ensure NDAs with all collaborators include non-reverse-engineering clauses. *(Marks distribution: 7 marks = 3 for (a) legal overview + 4 for (b) structured case analysis with statute references)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Frequency KTU Board Exam Topics** ### **Core Conceptual Points:** - **ELSI Triad:** Ethical + Legal + Social — all three must be addressed in any Industry 4.0 deployment discussion. - **Five Ethical Pillars:** Transparency, Accountability, Fairness, Privacy, Human-Centricity. - **Human-in-the-Loop (HITL):** Mandatory design principle for autonomous Industry 4.0 systems per KTU 2024 outcomes. - **GDPR Article 17:** Right to be Forgotten — frequently asked 3-mark question. - **DPDPA 2023:** India's data protection law — relevant to all case studies set in Indian context. - **Digital Divide Index (DDI):** Formula is $\text{DDI} = \frac{1}{m}\sum \frac{A_j^{\max} - A_j^{\text{actual}}}{A_j^{\max}}$ — remember the normalized form. - **Automation Risk Threshold:** $P(\text{Auto}) \geq 0.7$ = High Risk classification. - **Network Exposure Risk:** $\text{NER} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ — applied to IoT cybersecurity. - **Bias Fairness Index:** $\Delta_{\text{bias}} = \vert P(Y=1 \mid S=0) - P(Y=1 \mid S=1) \vert$ — must be $\leq 0.05$ for compliance. - **Liability Chain:** Manufacturer → Integrator → Vendor → Operator → End User. - **IEC 62443** is the international cybersecurity standard for Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS). - **IEEE Global Initiative** on Ethics of AI emphasizes **"Meaningful Human Control"** as a non-negotiable principle. - **Article 14 of Indian Constitution** + **DPDPA Section 8** are the most-cited legal provisions for AI fairness in KTU exams. ### **Examiner's Favorite 14-Mark Question Pattern:** - Part (a): Theory/Framework explanation with **3-4 named principles/laws**. - Part (b): A **case study** set in Indian/Kerala industry context, requiring ethical + legal + social analysis. ### **Top 5 Mistakes to Avoid:** 1. ✗ Writing generic answers without Industry 4.0 context. 2. ✗ Forgetting to cite specific laws or regulations. 3. ✗ Ignoring the CO4 mapping (societal/environmental context). 4. ✗ Confusing Automation Risk with Digital Divide Index. 5. ✗ Skipping the numerical/quantitative aspect in 14-mark answers — always include at least one formula or metric.
